meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve tutorial hell (Beginner Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome tutorial hell. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS Pricing to Solve Tutorial Hell
content.seo_description: Learn how to implement SaaS pricing and overcome tutorial hell. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code for your first real-world app.
content.answer_block: Tutorial hell ends when you stop watching and start shipping. By building a functional SaaS pricing model, you transition from a consumer to a creator. We will use Antigravity to scaffold the logic and Claude Code to implement the subscription architecture, transforming your code into a professional, revenue-ready product.
content.intro:
The screen glows with the fifth 'To-Do list' tutorial of the week. You feel productive, yet the sinking realization hits: you are just rearranging syntax, not building a business. Tutorial hell is the graveyard of ambition, where developers get stuck in the infinite loop of passive consumption.

Today, we break the cycle. We are moving beyond CRUD apps and into the architecture of value. By integrating a real SaaS pricing engine, you are no longer just coding; you are designing a system that handles logic, state, and user intent. It is time to stop watching and start architecting.

steps.title: Define the Value Architecture
steps.description: Before writing code, define the tiers. We are creating a 'Free', 'Pro', and 'Enterprise' structure to understand how logic dictates user access.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a schema for a SaaS pricing model with three tiers: Free (limited requests), Pro (unlimited), and Enterprise (custom). Include fields for price_id, feature_limit, and status.

steps.title: Bootstrap the Logic Layer
steps.description: Use Antigravity to generate the backend logic that validates user subscription status against their API requests.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a middleware function that checks a user's subscription status from the database and restricts access if the request limit is exceeded.

steps.title: Integrate Stripe Checkout Flow
steps.description: Implement the checkout session logic. This is the bridge between your code and real-world utility.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a Stripe checkout session handler in TypeScript that accepts a price_id and returns a URL for the user to complete payment.

steps.title: Build the Webhook Listener
steps.description: A pricing model is useless without state management. You need a webhook to handle subscription updates when a payment succeeds.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a Node.js webhook handler for Stripe events, specifically 'checkout.session.completed' and 'customer.subscription.updated'.

steps.title: Deploy and Validate
steps.description: Test the full loop: from selecting a tier to updating the database state.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a suite of unit tests for the subscription validation middleware, mocking the Stripe event payload.

pro_tips: Always build your pricing logic as a decoupled service; it makes swapping payment providers painless later.
Use environment-specific secret management from day one; never hardcode your Stripe keys.
Implement 'Graceful Degradation'—if the pricing API fails, default the user to a limited free tier rather than locking them out entirely.
Log every subscription event to a separate database table for easier debugging of payment issues.
Focus on the 'Happy Path' first; you can add complex edge case handling after the primary flow is functional.
content.next_steps: Now that your pricing engine is active, integrate a dashboard where users can view their usage metrics and upgrade their plans directly.
content.conversion_block: You have the tools, the architecture, and the logic. Don't let this knowledge sit in a repo—deploy it, share it, and watch your portfolio transform from 'tutorial' to 'SaaS'. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
